02 · Context

Entering a System Too Large to Fully Understand

In 2021, I joined Pulse Operations as a Junior UI Designer within a large product team at Prudential Vietnam. At the time, I knew very little about insurance operations, workflow systems, enterprise UX, or design systems.

To me, Pulse Operations was simply "a massive internal platform with too much data, too many screens, and overwhelming complexity."

My day-to-day work was straightforward: receive requirements from BAs, design interfaces, hand off to developers, then move to the next screen.

"Looking back from 2026, I now realize: Pulse Operations was never just an admin dashboard. It was an enterprise-scale operational platform orchestrating thousands of insurance workflows behind the scenes every day."

What I didn't understand yet

  • Why the system existed and its business purpose
  • How insurance operations actually worked end-to-end
  • What pressures operations users faced every single day
  • What each task or workflow status truly represented
  • The relationship between UI decisions and operational outcomes

Platform capabilities

  • Operational tasks & approval flows
  • Policy records & customer data management
  • Workflow transitions & audit trails
  • Permissions & role-based access control
  • SLA monitoring & operational tracking
03 · Problem

Inconsistency Everywhere

The most obvious issue in 2021 was interface inconsistency. Different parts of the system felt like they were designed by different generations of teams.

01
🔀 Inconsistent Spacing & Typography
Different parts of the system used inconsistent spacing rules and typographic hierarchies, making the interface hard to scan.
02
🧩 Fragmented Component Styles
Components evolved independently. Buttons, form fields, and tables had no shared implementation reference across teams.
03
⚡ Mismatched Interaction Patterns
New features continuously drifted away from older ones, creating unpredictable behavior for users navigating across modules.
04
📈 Compounding Design Debt
Rapid system scaling without strong foundations led to fragmented operational experiences and increasing maintenance burden.
🔍
Rapid Scaling Without Foundation
The system grew faster than the design foundation could support, creating compounding inconsistency with every new feature.
🧠
Cognitive Overload for Users
Inconsistent UI patterns increased cognitive load for operations staff processing hundreds of tasks per day.
⚙️
Developer Implementation Gap
Without a shared design language, developers had no consistent reference, causing UI drift between design and production.
04 · Enterprise UX

What I Later Realized About Enterprise UX

Years later, after learning more about Product Thinking and Enterprise UX, I started seeing Pulse Operations differently. The real problem wasn't whether the UI looked "good" or "bad".

The deeper issue was rapid system scaling without strong foundations, growing workflow complexity, fragmented operational experiences, and increasing cognitive load for internal users.

"In enterprise products, UI is not decoration. It is part of the operational infrastructure."

An inconsistent spacing system reduces scanability. Unclear statuses create operational mistakes. Unstandardized components slow down both designers and developers.

Enterprise UX measured by

⚡ Operational Clarity95%
🔄 Workflow Efficiency88%
🎯 Predictability82%
🧠 Reduced Cognitive Load90%
📈 Scalability85%

NOT measured by

Visual novelty · Trendy animations · Aesthetic-first decisions

05 · System Thinking

The System Beneath the Interface

While working on the product, I gradually realized that underneath the UI existed a much larger operational system — a Workflow Orchestration Platform.

I was not involved in business strategy or product decision-making. However, while working on the product, I gradually realized that underneath the UI existed a much larger operational system.

Pulse Operations functioned as a Workflow Orchestration Platform revolving around operational tasks, policy records, approval flows, permissions, audit logs, and operational tracking.

"Each task on the My Tasks screen actually represented a business workflow step — not just a data entry point. The platform wasn't managing data. It was managing operational states."

Each task represented

Task Type 01
Document Verification
Verifying customer identity documents against policy records in real time.
Task Type 02
Policy Information Update
Processing requests to update customer address, phone, and identity documents.
Task Type 03
Claims Processing
Managing insurance claims from submission through verification to approval.
Task Type 04
Approval & Escalation
Routing complex or high-risk cases through multi-level approval chains.
06 · Design System

Building My First Design System

No one asked me to build a Design System. There was no roadmap initiative for it. But I had a strong intuition that without rebuilding the foundation, the system would continue becoming more fragmented.

To better understand how large systems should be structured, I spent significant time studying Material Design — not to imitate Google's UI, but because it was the first time I saw design tokens, component hierarchies, reusable patterns, interaction states, spacing systems, and documentation standards organized as a complete system.

"That was the first moment I shifted from screen-level thinking to system-level thinking."

I proposed a one-month UI Library revamp initiative and rebuilt typography scales, spacing systems, button variants, form patterns, tables, reusable components, and layout consistency. The updated library was gradually applied across the product.

Aa
Typography
Standardized type hierarchy
📐
Spacing
4pt grid system
🔘
Buttons
All states & variants
📝
Forms
Input & validation
📊
Tables
Data-rich patterns
🧩
Components
Reusable & documented
🎨
Color Tokens
Semantic system
📱
Layout
Grid & containers

⏱ Timeline

One-month UI Library revamp · Gradual rollout across product

07 · Constraints

Designing Within a Live Enterprise Environment

One thing I later learned: refactoring a live enterprise product is fundamentally different from redesigning a concept app. Pulse Operations was actively used by operations teams every day.

I had no authority over the platform architecture. But I learned that every UI decision inside enterprise systems has operational dependencies — and that is what makes enterprise UX significantly more complex than visual interface design alone.

"Every UI decision inside enterprise systems has operational dependencies."
🔄
Workflows Cannot Be Disrupted
Operations teams rely on the platform continuously. Any UI change must be designed around zero downtime to active workflows.
📦
Backward Compatibility Matters
Existing processes must remain intact. Rollout needs to happen incrementally to avoid regression.
🤝
Developer Collaboration is Critical
Close collaboration with engineers ensures implementation fidelity and prevents the design-to-code quality gap from widening.
08 · Outcomes

Results & Honest Limitations

To be fully honest: I did not measure any metrics in 2021. No usability testing, no workflow analytics, no quantitative validation. This is something I clearly recognize as a limitation today.

⚠️

Honest Limitation

No usability testing · No workflow analytics · No task completion tracking · No quantitative validation. These are gaps I now actively work to address in every subsequent project.

✨
Interface Cohesion
After rollout, the interface became noticeably more cohesive. Newer features started sharing the same visual language.
🔧
Cleaner Handoff
Developer handoff became clearer and more structured. Implementation consistency improved across teams.
👥
Positive Response
PMs and internal users responded positively to improved visual consistency and more predictable interaction patterns.
📚
Design Language Adoption
Newer features started sharing the same visual language, preventing further design drift across the product.
🏆
Year-end Award
Received Prudential Vietnam's Year-end Contribution Award 2021 for helping establish a stronger operational foundation.
📐
Scalable Foundation
The design system provided a foundation for onboarding new designers and maintaining consistency as the team grew.

End-to-End Workflow

Follow a complete customer request through the AI-powered 2026 operations platform — from inbox to approval and audit trail.

Home — AI-Powered Workload Overview
The redesigned dashboard introduces a live Workload Overview panel with SLA Risk Alerts, AI Assistant sidebar with smart prioritization, and Individual Performance metrics. The agent immediately sees what needs attention.
10 · Comparison

2021 vs 2026: Home Dashboard

A direct side-by-side of the same core screen — from a static data table to an AI-assisted operational cockpit. The 2021 screen appears exclusively here for comparison context.

Original Dashboard 2021
❌ Pain Points
—No task prioritization or urgency signals visible
—Static flat table — user must manually assess each task
—No SLA monitoring or proactive risk alerts
—Quick Actions sidebar as the primary nav shortcut
—No AI assistance, no automation support
—High cognitive load — all triage done manually
AI-Powered Cockpit 2026
✅ Key Improvements
+Priority column with directional risk indicators (High↑, Low↓)
+Live Workload Overview — SLA Risk Alert counter front and center
+AI Assistant panel with real-time workload summaries
+SLA Performance donut chart — On time / Warning / Overdue
+Individual Performance metrics with trend deltas
+AI-powered smart prioritization reduces manual triage
Static Task Dashboard
→
5 years · AI-native redesign
Operational Decision Cockpit
→
AI-Powered Cockpit

3
Panel layout zones
Real-time
SLA Risk Monitoring
0

11 · AI Vision

Reimagining Pulse Operations in 2026

This section is not about what I did in 2021. It reflects how I would rethink Pulse Operations today — evolving from task management to operational decision support.

The 2020–2021 version reflected a traditional operational dashboard: data-heavy interfaces, manual workflows, status-driven interactions, and human-dependent processing.

If redesigned today, the platform would evolve into an AI-assisted operations platform — not just displaying information, but helping users understand what requires attention next.

"The goal is not replacing humans. The goal is reducing decision fatigue, repetitive analysis, and information overload — giving operations staff the clarity to focus on judgment-intensive work."
01
📄
Document Summarization
AI automatically reads and summarizes supporting documents, extracting key fields and flagging discrepancies before the agent reviews.
02
🔍
Anomaly Detection
Real-time pattern recognition identifies unusual requests, duplicate submissions, or suspicious document inconsistencies indicating fraud risk.
03
⚡
SLA Risk Prediction
Predictive models flag tasks approaching SLA violation before breach, enabling proactive routing and workload rebalancing.
04
🎯
Next-Step Recommendations
Context-aware suggestions guide agents through non-standard cases, recommending the most appropriate action based on case history.
05
📊
Smart Prioritization
Dynamic task ordering based on SLA urgency, customer VIP status, case complexity, and available agent capacity in real time.
06
🛡️
Fraud Pattern Detection
Cross-referencing documents against historical fraud signatures and external identity verification sources to protect against falsified claims.
🚀 Vision 2026

From Static Dashboard to
Operational Cockpit

A workspace where users can prioritize work, receive AI insights, monitor bottlenecks, manage workflows in context, and gain operational visibility in real time.

🎯
Prioritize Work
AI surfaces the most critical tasks based on SLA, risk, and impact
🔍
Monitor Bottlenecks
Real-time visibility into workflow health and team capacity
🤖
AI Copilot
Contextual assistance that reduces manual analysis burden
12 · Reflection

What This Project Taught Me

"Pulse Operations was the first project that taught me: complex systems are often much larger than what designers initially see on the screen. Great enterprise UX is rarely about visual attention — it is about helping organizations operate more effectively within systems so complex that end users may never even realize they exist."
Role at the time
Junior UI Designer
Company
Prudential Vietnam
Recognition
Year-end Contribution Award 2021
Key Shift
Screen Design → System Design

What I Grew Into

Systems Thinking Operational UX Workflow Design Design Governance Design Systems Scalability Thinking Component Architecture Enterprise UX Patterns Cross-functional Collaboration Design Token Systems Developer Handoff SLA-Aware Design Cognitive Load Reduction Product Thinking Foundations

What I Was Never Involved In

  • Business strategy or product decision-making
  • Platform architecture decisions
  • Quantitative success measurement
  • User research or usability testing
  • Workflow analytics or task completion tracking

What I Would Do Differently Today

  • Define measurable success criteria before design begins
  • Conduct contextual inquiry with operations staff
  • Map the full operational workflow before touching the UI
  • Instrument key interactions for post-launch analytics
  • Run structured usability tests with real workflow scenarios
  • Frame design system work as a product investment, not cleanup
pulse°

Pulse Operations — Case Study by a Designer in Transition
